frameworks.name: context-dependent evidence accumulation
frameworks.one_line: network solves contextual selection-and-accumulation via three identifiable components

frameworks.name: decomposable network theory
frameworks.one_line: decomposition into testable components admitting multiple valid combinations

core_question: How do neural circuits implement context-dependent selection and accumulation of evidence for flexible decision-making?
key_assumptions: task assumptions are supported by both monkey and rat data
context-dependent computation reduces to three defined components
different individuals may combine the components differently
manipulations: contextual cue determining which stimulus stream is relevant
individual subject identity (heterogeneity across many trained rats)
key_findings: mathematical proof that a network can solve the task via three defined components
existing electrophysiology and modeling data are compatible with multiple component combinations
neural and behavioral analyses reveal substantial heterogeneity across rats despite uniformly good performance
theory's predicted link between behavioral and neural signatures is robustly supported
interpretations: context-dependent decision-making is implemented by a small set of components that subjects mix in individually different ways
behavioral and neural variability are jointly explained by the same component-level framework
